text:
The catalytic C–H borylation of primary and secondary sp³ C–H bonds is a well-established method for introducing diverse functionalities to a broad spectrum of organic molecules.1 However, catalytic activation of the more challenging, tertiary sp³ C–H bonds has been achieved with minimal success under normal conditions. Herein, we disclose that, for the first time, the bridgehead tertiary C–H bonds of bicyclo[1.1.1]pentanes (BCPs), bicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (BCHs) and heterobicyclo[2.1.1]hexanes (XBCHs) can be selectively borylated with high efficiency and selectivity in a broadly applicable iridium catalytic process. This catalyst system employs [Ir(COD)OMe]₂ in the presence of 2-methylphenanthroline and affords over 35 benchmark systems (≈100% selective) where only the bridgehead tertiary C–H is borylated; with relevance to the late stage modification of complex pharmaceuticals and the synthesis of new bicyclic functionalised building blocks.

The tertiary centers of the tris-cyclopropanated hexarene substrate were reported to undergo C–H activation more easily than the non-activated C–H bond of benzene under the same conditions. They ascribed this unique reactivity due to high s-character of C–H bonds at the tertiary centers that arose from the significant bridgehead strain, which made the rates of C–H activation similar to that of cyclopropanes and greater than those of the non-activated alkyl C–H bonds. Further studies revealed rates and mechanism of substrate activation using the Ru catalyst, and identified transition states, energies, and species with the aid of computational modeling, while estimating C–H activation and elimination energies. They demonstrated that reversible cleavage of C–H bonds from tertiary centers of the tris-cyclopropanated hexarene proceeded with moderate energy barriers and that the overall reaction turned over by an isomerization of the seven-coordinate Ir(V) complex preceding the C–B bond reductive elimination. The resultant boronic esters were highly stable in a three-dimensional bridgehead framework that is otherwise difficult to attain because the energy associated with their formation, in general, involves multiple steps, high energy intermediates or strained bonds.
explanation: The text uses authentic chemistry terminology and specifics that resemble a real paper, suggesting it could be human-written, but certain phrasing and unusual substrate descriptions leave room for AI authorship.
